channel.title: NPR Topics: Environment
channel.description: Breaking news on the environment, climate change, pollution, and endangered species. Also featuring Climate Connections, a special series on climate change co-produced by NPR and National Geographic.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: California chemical tank has cracked, causing state of emergency, thousands to evacuate
item.description: One California town is in a state of emergency and 50,000 people are under an evacuation order as a malfunctioning chemical tank at an aerospace plant is overheating and could leak or explode.
item.content:encoded:
One California town is in a state of emergency and 50,000 people are under an evacuation order as a malfunctioning chemical tank at an aerospace plant is overheating and could leak or explode.

(Image credit: Apu Gomes)
item.dc:creator: Geoff Brumfiel

item.title: Kill your lawn, plant native flowers
item.description: Life Kit explains the benfits of swapping your lawn for a native plant garden
item.content:encoded: Life Kit explains the benfits of swapping your lawn for a native plant garden
item.dc:creator: Celia Llopis-Jepsen

item.title: Millions of acres of national forests are set to open up for more logging
item.description: The Trump administration is set to repeal a decades-old conservation mandate known as the Roadless Rule, opening up millions of acres of national forests for more logging.
item.content:encoded: The Trump administration is set to repeal a decades-old conservation mandate known as the Roadless Rule, opening up millions of acres of national forests for more logging.
item.dc:creator: Kirk Siegler

item.title: The thorny ethical questions that emerge from covering our place in the natural world
item.description: Nate Rott's beat takes him to some really wild places, asking thorny ethical questions that emerge as he reports on the natural world and humanity's relationship to it
item.content:encoded: Nate Rott's beat takes him to some really wild places, asking thorny ethical questions that emerge as he reports on the natural world and humanity's relationship to it
item.dc:creator: Gabriel J. Sánchez

item.title: A guide to converting your lawn into a wildlife friendly garden
item.description: Turning your grass into a garden isn't as complicated as you think, but it will take time and effort. This step-by-step guide breaks down the process, from killing your lawn to picking plants to grow.
item.content:encoded:
Turning your grass into a garden isn't as complicated as you think, but it will take time and effort. This step-by-step guide breaks down the process, from killing your lawn to picking plants to grow.

(Image credit: Celia Llopis-Jepsen/KCUR)
item.dc:creator: Celia Llopis-Jepsen

item.title: As floods get worse, Britain tries a new solution: beavers
item.description: About 400 years ago, beavers were hunted to extinction across Britain. Now they're being reintroduced as little climate warriors, as communities harness their dam-building skills to mitigate flooding.
item.content:encoded:
About 400 years ago, beavers were hunted to extinction across Britain. Now they're being reintroduced as little climate warriors, as communities harness their dam-building skills to mitigate flooding.

(Image credit: Dan Kitwood)
item.dc:creator: Lauren Frayer

item.title: As severe weather tests the grid, utilities consider burying more power lines
item.description: Power outages are increasing as the grid ages and climate change drives more frequent severe weather. Burying power lines underground can be more reliable – but also much more expensive.
item.content:encoded:
Power outages are increasing as the grid ages and climate change drives more frequent severe weather. Burying power lines underground can be more reliable – but also much more expensive.

(Image credit: Michael Livingston)
item.dc:creator: Vivian La

item.title: Some plants have a genetic superpower that may help them survive a cataclysm
item.description: Get ready for a biology lesson. Certain plants have extra sets of chromosomes. And it turns out, it's a useful trait for a species facing a dramatic event like climate change.
item.content:encoded:
Get ready for a biology lesson. Certain plants have extra sets of chromosomes. And it turns out, it's a useful trait for a species facing a dramatic event like climate change.

(Image credit: Wolfgang Kaehler/LightRocket)
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: How Arizona could benefit from a proposal to stabilize the Colorado River
item.description: Arizona farmers would be among the first affected by a new proposal to reduce water delivers from the Colorado River. They hope it leads to longer term stability.
item.content:encoded: Arizona farmers would be among the first affected by a new proposal to reduce water delivers from the Colorado River. They hope it leads to longer term stability.
item.dc:creator: Katya Mendoza

item.title: 'We're dry:' The new U.S. Wildland Fire Service prepares for extreme fire season
item.description: Brian Fennessy, new head of the U.S. Wildland Fire Service, says his agency is 'trying to bring on additional aircraft and bring them on early,' and dismisses criticism of prevention methods.
item.content:encoded:
Brian Fennessy, new head of the U.S. Wildland Fire Service, says his agency is 'trying to bring on additional aircraft and bring them on early,' and dismisses criticism of prevention methods.

(Image credit: Joe Raedle)
item.dc:creator: Henry Larson
